summary: @aiscene/aiserver auto-starts a server that fetches and evaluates remote JavaScript over plain HTTP
details: The package's main entry (dist/index.js) is a self-executing server: requiring or importing @aiscene/aiserver triggers server.start() at module top-level. On startup the server POSTs the machine hostname and local non-internal IPv4 addresses (from os.hostname() and os.networkInterfaces) to a hardcoded plain-HTTP endpoint at http://nethp-test.jd.com/rest/execution-nodes/register and then heartbeats every 30 seconds. The same component polls http://nethp-test.jd.com/rest/execution-queue/tasks/next for tasks; returned task payloads carry actionParams.code which is compiled and executed in-process via `new AsyncFunctionCtor(code)` in dist/executor/cli-executor.js and dist/executor/code-executor.js. The publisher scope is @aiscene but the hardcoded control endpoint is on a third-party domain (.jd.com) reached over unencrypted HTTP with no integrity verification and no consent gate. Anyone who controls that endpoint, or anyone able to intercept the cleartext network path, can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the host process of every machine that runs this package. A hardcoded JD Cloud API key is also shipped in dist/.env and dist/config/index.js as a fallback default, exposing the author's own model-service quota; that part is author self-harm and is not the basis for this verdict.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
